title: How to Secure Your Amazon Account: Complete 2026 Guide
excerpt: Your Amazon account stores your home address, credit cards, purchase history, and access to dozens of connected services. This guide covers every step to lock it down — from setting a strong password and enabling two-step verification to auditing connected apps and spotting phishing emails.
content:
Why Your Amazon Account Is a High-Value Target

Your Amazon account stores more sensitive data than most people realize: your home address, credit card numbers, purchase history, saved login credentials for Alexa, and often access to Amazon Web Services, Amazon Pay, or Kindle purchases. For many households it's also tied to Prime Video, Audible, and Amazon Kids — making a breach a family-wide problem, not just a personal one.

Attackers know this. Amazon accounts are routinely targeted through credential stuffing (trying username/password combinations from other breaches), phishing emails impersonating Amazon order confirmations, and account takeover scams where fraudsters use unauthorized purchases and then intercept the shipping. Once inside, attackers can order expensive items to reshipping addresses, sell or abuse your gift card balance, change account recovery settings to lock you out, and access stored payment methods.

The good news: securing your Amazon account thoroughly takes less than 30 minutes, and the steps below cover every meaningful attack surface.

Step 1: Set a Strong, Unique Amazon Password

The most common way Amazon accounts get compromised is credential stuffing — attackers take username/password combinations from other breached sites and test them automatically against Amazon. If you use the same password on Amazon as you've used anywhere else, this is your highest-priority fix. Change it now.

Your Amazon password should be at least 16 characters, completely random, and used only for Amazon. Use our free password generator to create one: set the length to 20 characters, include uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and symbols. The result will be something like Kx7$mNp2#qRw9vYt4!Lz — impossible to remember on its own, which is correct. Store it in a password manager like NordPass or 1Password and let them handle autofill.

To change your Amazon password: go to Amazon.com → Account & Lists → Account → Login & security → Password → Edit. You'll need to verify your identity. After changing it, sign out all other active sessions (there's an option for this during the security settings flow).

Step 2: Enable Two-Step Verification

Two-step verification (Amazon's term for 2FA) means that even if someone has your password, they can't log in without a second code. It's free, takes five minutes to set up, and blocks the vast majority of automated account takeover attempts.

Amazon supports two second-factor methods: authenticator apps (preferred) and SMS. To set it up: Account & Lists → Account → Login & security → Two-Step Verification (2SV) Settings → Get Started. Select "Authenticator App," then scan the QR code with Google Authenticator, Authy, or Microsoft Authenticator. Save the backup codes Amazon provides — store them in your password manager or print them and keep them somewhere physically secure.

If you already have SMS-based 2SV, consider upgrading to an authenticator app. SMS is vulnerable to SIM swapping attacks, where an attacker convinces your mobile carrier to transfer your phone number to a SIM they control, intercepting all your text messages. An authenticator app generates codes locally on your device and is immune to this attack.

Amazon also lets you designate trusted devices that don't require a second factor every login. Be selective: only mark devices you personally own and control as trusted. Never mark a shared, public, or work device as trusted.

Step 3: Review Saved Payment Methods and Addresses

An attacker who gets into your Amazon account will typically try to order expensive, resellable items — electronics, gift cards, Amazon credit — to a different shipping address before you notice. Reviewing and tightening your payment and address settings limits the damage window.

Go to Account & Lists → Account → Your Addresses and delete any addresses you no longer use or don't recognize. Unfamiliar addresses are a red flag for prior unauthorized access. Similarly, go to Account & Lists → Account → Payment methods and remove any cards you no longer use. Consider whether you actually need a credit card stored on Amazon at all — some security-conscious users prefer to enter payment details fresh for each purchase and store nothing persistently.

If you use Amazon Pay on third-party sites, review those authorizations too: Account & Lists → Account → Amazon Pay → Merchant agreements. Revoke access for any merchants you no longer shop at or don't recognize. Each active merchant authorization is an additional attack surface.

Step 4: Audit Connected Devices and Third-Party Apps

Your Amazon account may be connected to Alexa devices, Fire tablets, Kindle e-readers, smart TVs, and dozens of third-party apps and services via Login with Amazon. Each connection is a potential pivot point for an attacker. Regularly reviewing and pruning these connections reduces your exposure.

To review Alexa and connected devices: Account & Lists → Account → Content & Devices → Devices. Deregister any devices you no longer own or recognize. If you've ever sold an Amazon device, confirm it was deregistered from your account before the sale.

To review third-party apps: Account & Lists → Account → Login with Amazon. This shows every service that can authenticate using your Amazon credentials. Revoke access for any apps you no longer use or don't recognize. Also check Appstore subscriptions and active memberships for any that don't look familiar — unauthorized subscriptions are occasionally added after account compromise.

Step 5: Set Up Purchase Notifications and Monitor Activity

Real-time purchase notifications give you an early warning system that works even if an attacker changes your password and locks you out before you notice. The sooner you detect unauthorized activity, the better your options for disputing charges and recovering your account.

Enable email notifications for every order: Account & Lists → Account → Communication preferences → Shipment notifications. Make sure these go to an email address you check regularly — ideally not the same email that's used as your Amazon login, so an attacker who compromises your Amazon email doesn't also suppress the notification.

You should also set up purchase notifications through your credit card issuer for any card linked to Amazon. Most major credit cards and banks offer real-time push notifications for transactions above a threshold you set ($0 works well). This creates a second, independent notification channel that's outside Amazon's control entirely. Review your Amazon order history periodically — at least monthly — for any orders you didn't place.

Step 6: Watch for Amazon Phishing Emails

Amazon is one of the most impersonated brands in phishing campaigns. Fake "order confirmation" emails, "account suspended" notices, and "verify your payment method" messages are sent at massive scale and are often visually indistinguishable from real Amazon emails. The payload is always the same: get you to click a link and enter your credentials on a fake Amazon login page.

The reliable defense is to treat all email links with suspicion. If you get an email claiming to be from Amazon about an order, a problem with your account, or a security alert, do not click any link in that email. Instead, open a new browser tab and navigate to amazon.com directly, then check your orders, messages, and account alerts there. If the issue is real, it will show up in your account. If it doesn't, the email was a phishing attempt.

Check the actual sender email address (not just the display name) for any Amazon email that asks you to take action. Real Amazon emails come from @amazon.com domains. Anything from @amazon-security.com, @amazon-notification.net, @amazon.co-verify.com, or any variation with extra words or different TLDs is a phishing attempt. Report it and delete it without clicking anything.

Amazon Account Security Checklist

  • Password: Unique, 16+ characters, generated with a password generator, stored in a password manager
  • Two-step verification: Enabled with an authenticator app (not just SMS)
  • Backup codes: Saved securely in your password manager or printed
  • Trusted devices: Only personal, controlled devices marked as trusted — never shared or public devices
  • Saved addresses: Reviewed — removed old or unrecognized addresses
  • Payment methods: Only cards you actively use stored; old cards removed
  • Amazon Pay merchants: Audited — revoked access for merchants you no longer use
  • Connected devices: Deregistered any devices you no longer own
  • Login with Amazon apps: Audited — revoked access for unused or unrecognized apps
  • Purchase notifications: Set up via Amazon email AND credit card push alerts
  • Phishing awareness: Never clicking email links — always navigating directly to amazon.com

Recommended Tools

A password manager is the foundation of Amazon account security — it lets you set a unique, strong password without needing to remember it. We recommend NordPass (zero-knowledge encryption, free tier, breach monitoring) or 1Password for families who want to share passwords and licenses across household members.

For identity monitoring — alerts if your email, payment info, or personal data appears in new breach databases — consider LifeLock, which offers dark web monitoring alongside identity theft protection.

See our full security tools guide for more recommendations on protecting your digital accounts.
